attributes.stem:
A 65-year-old man presents to the neurology clinic with a rapidly progressive cognitive decline over the past few months. His family reports personality changes, memory loss, and myoclonic jerks, which have become more frequent and pronounced in the past few weeks. He is a retired farmer and has no significant medical history, with no history of head trauma or surgery. Upon further inquiry, his wife mentions occasional episodes of confusion and agitation during the night, as well as visual hallucinations. She notes that he has become increasingly withdrawn and disinterested in his usual activities. The patient's grandfather reportedly suffered similar symptoms, shortly before his death, although the family are not aware of any formal diagnosis.

On examination, he displays ataxia, particularly evident during tandem gait testing, and primitive reflexes such as grasp reflex. His speech is dysarthric and he has difficulty with rapid alternating movements. There is no evidence of focal weakness or sensory deficits. Initial laboratory investigations, including complete blood count, electrolytes, liver function tests, and thyroid function tests, are within normal limits. Electroencephalogram (EEG) shows generalised periodic sharp wave complexes. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) reveals bilateral symmetrical hyperintensities in the basal ganglia, particularly involving the caudate nucleus, putamen and medial thalamus, which is described in the report as a 'hockey-stick' sign.
attributes.stemQuestion: Given the constellation of symptoms and findings, which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
The probable diagnosis is Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease (CJD). This is a rapidly progressive neurodegenerative disorder caused by prion protein misfolding. Risk factors include farming history, history of psychosis, family history or CJD and possibly prior surgeries. Common features include rapid cognitive decline, myoclonus, ataxia, dysarthria. Characteristic MRI findings include bilateral symmetrical hyperintensities in the basal ganglia, known as the "pulvinar sign", which in combination with similar hyperintensities in the medial thalamus form the "hockey-stick sign". Similar MRI findings may also be present in conditions such as Wernicke Encephalopathy. EEG may show generalised periodic sharp wave complexes, further supporting the diagnosis.

Vascular dementia is characterised by cognitive decline due to cerebrovascular disease, often with a stepwise progression. While vascular dementia can present with various cognitive and neurological deficits, the rapid progression, myoclonic jerks, and characteristic MRI findings make this diagnosis less likely.

Normal pressure hydrocephalus (NPH) typically presents with a triad of symptoms: gait disturbances, urinary incontinence, and cognitive impairment. While cognitive decline can occur in NPH, the presence of myoclonic jerks, primitive reflexes, dysarthria, and characteristic MRI findings make this diagnosis less likely.

Huntington's disease is an autosomal dominant neurodegenerative disorder characterised by motor dysfunction, cognitive decline, and psychiatric symptoms. However, the presence of myoclonic jerks, rapid progression of symptoms, and absence of a family history of Huntington disease make this diagnosis less likely.

Parkinson's disease primarily presents with motor symptoms such as tremor, rigidity, and bradykinesia. While cognitive impairment may occur in later stages, the presence of myoclonic jerks, rapid cognitive decline, and characteristic MRI findings make Parkinson disease less likely in this case.
learningPoints: Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease is a rare but important neurological disease and its cardinal manifestations are rapidly progressive mental deterioration, behavioural abnormalities and myoclonus.
references: Ninds.nih.gov. 2020. Creutzfeldt-Jakob Disease Fact Sheet | National Institute Of Neurological Disorders And Stroke. [online] Available at: <https://www.ninds.nih.gov/disorders/patient-caregiver-education/fact-sheets/creutzfeldt-jakob-disease-fact-sheet> [Accessed 10 September 2020].
Appleby BS, ML C. Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease. UpToDate. https://www.uptodate.com/contents/creutzfeldt-jakob-disease
